item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn. The end really hit me. Upvoted.

Also, I wonder if a term for the scenario this caused is the “☿K-Class “World of Babel” Global-Incomprehension Scenario” from the Doomsday 2018 contest. A lot of this seems to resemble it, with both end results being that >75% of the population falls (presumably) to this, except in 3449’s case, it looks like it snuffed out all of humanity.

item.title: ... wow +1
item.content:encoded: That was a wild ride. I think this, End of Life XK stories, and Fifth Church (GoI) are my favorite canons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I didn't include this directly in the article, there is this link in the author post that redirects you to transcriptions of all of the non-text parts of the SCP. I don't put them in the main article for narrative reasons, but they do exist.

I have also just now added that link to the info box, so it is accessible from the main article. However I don't want to make it a diegetic element of the original article because that weakens the blow of those iterations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm on a computer without sound. Is there a way for me (and for that matter, any deaf readers of the site) to learn anything about what's going on here? Just basic accessibility dictates the should be some alternative. :/

ETA: I mean, I get that "…and then, everything went to hell". But I presume those audio clips have some reason for being there, and that I'm missing something by not being able to listen to them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, that's one hell of a way to set up your face reveal.

On a much more serious note, this one *really* got me good. "Oh, that's pretty quirky… oh hey, there's more!" turns into "Shit, this is gonna go real bad." with pinpoint timing. Seriously, the pacing is excellent. I'm also a big fan of the overall variety — I think it helps a lot to hammer in the hopeless nature of the situation.

Great job and thanks to everybody involved in this scip, I'll probably be passing it around for a while as a good example of using multiple formats. Pray we don't lose shrugging and waving any time soon.

item.title: Minor grammar fix
item.content:encoded:
I made a few small changes to your SCP

Removed second "the"
Added a "," after "also"
Both in segment 4
Thank you everyone for you reading! I invested more time and effort into this piece than any other I've put on the site so far, so I'm glad you've all enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OOF. This is a very well-made article, you can tell right away that a lot of effort and hard work were put into it.

My favorite thing about this skip is the ending. I would never expect it to end this way when I clicked "reconnect". I thought there will be something else there. Some additional info, some solution, some edits telling you that it all was a memetic hazard or smth. But no, there is nothing after that last revision. And there couldn't be anything, because it's the end.

I can't believe that I've seen this article before but didn't read it, it was a big mistake. +1'ed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Turns out our last word arent gonna be on our deathbed but rather.. somewhere by ourselves when we forget how to use them. Damn. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another irreversible end-of-the-world scenario? What should this one be called?

item.title: Awesome
item.content:encoded: First SCP Article I've seen with a video! Keep it up dude!

item.title: Translation?!
item.content:encoded:
So, as you can probably tell, this article is not exactly easy to translate, even if you do go through the transcripts of the audio logs. However, I found someone who made a korean translation. It's not like, on the korean scp wiki site, but I would like to at least leave a link to this in case its useful. Also because I find it incredible that someone actually took the time to go through, find the transcripts, port the video and do all kinds of things to make this translation. So yeah.

You can find said translation here

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! Also good point. I've added those redirects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there a multi-iteratiom ListPages based article I don't like?

This is powerful, terrifying and engaging. I think this succeeds at some things that I wanted but couldn't do with SCP-3519.

One minor technical thing: you might want to put redirects on the fragment pages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the kind words! I totally understand what you mean about the restraint bit, and I appreciate that being a little off… but I'm going to use this as an excuse to explain my creative choices anyways because that's fun!

Fun Rant About Kirby's Inner Thoughts!
Fyu 5aTG aBTTw 3-WT, I(()= taBTatta

I ride the emotional escalation harder here than in 3519 for two main reasons:

First, I felt that since I am going to be utilizing so many different forms of media, that it would make better use of said media formats to express the emotion. It would also be a little harder to sit through a much more restrained version of most of these logs. In fact, I remember getting early crit that the multimedia sections weren't panicky enough.

Secondly, I wasn't trying to use the break in tone/protocol as a kicker or stinger. 3519's main impact revolves around the incredibly sudden shift that happens at that last line, because that was your "oh shit" moment. Why I could've aimed for that here (and might've made for an interesting alternative structure), I instead opted for the "oh shit" moment of the Foundation truly losing. Yes, this isn't the first time the Foundation has lost in an SCP, but between the researcher's face going blank in the last video, and the little drop down noting that this was the final upload, my target was not to let the Foundation reaction be the final note, but the lack thereof.

The main reason for this is that the article is my answer to "what would happen if people stopped writing for the SCP Wiki" as an out-of-universe thing. So I wanted the final oomph to be just silence, because everything is gone. And it's easier (in my opinion) to contrast silence with high riding emotions and shouting than with restraint.

My working title for this piece was "End-Of-Story Scenario", because it was supposed to be the end of the Foundation's story. I changed it to "The Things Left Unsaid" though because I felt that it was a more poetic way to express what I want this to be about - all of the stories that can no longer be told, because there's no way left to tell them. They're just sitting in our heads, still left unsaid.

thank you for the excuse to rant btw

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I both enjoy the ambition of this piece and the manner in which it escalates; the subtle realization that the anomaly is 'evolving' to destroy written language — and the realization that if it can destroy sign language, that naturally means it can destroy body language (and even spoken language) is great. I also appreciate the amount of effort that must have gone into this.

What holds me back from upvoting is more a personal aesthetic than anything — there's a certain lack of restraint here, specifically in regards to the expressiveness of the researchers as things grow increasingly worse. For example, sirpudding's excellent SCP-3519 (These Quiet Days) handles a similar end-of-the-world scenario in a way that, while still being information-dense, manages to create a sense of subdued, "official" despair — which makes those moments when people 'break protocol' (particularly with the final line) so much stronger.

All in all, though, I liked the risks this piece took, and I thought the escalation was excellent — I certainly can't imagine downvoting this. And it looks like it's doing well regardless — which I think it certainly deserves. Great work, and I'm glad to see people experimenting with different formats for story-telling!

item.title: Re: Have I missed something?
item.content:encoded:
That makes sense!

Actually, that kinda sounds to me like it something that could work well if explicitly acknowledged, if you ever rerecord or rewrite this—I bet you could get some pathos out of "how could I have not thought to talk to anyone directly while I still had the chance?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I agree that this isn't the first article to go full end-of-the world (and most certainly won't be the last with DoomCon), there are some articles where the Foundation does manage to stop something.

SCP-2998 I would say is a pretty good example.

One could also make an argument that Document Recovered From The Marianas Trench follows that pattern as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. Could you give some examples of that please? I feel like it's just the opposite. I can think of a few articles where there's a thing that gets progressively worse and eventually destroys all humanity, but I can't think of anything where things look like they're going bad but eventually get resolved. So I feel like the latter would be the subversion, and I'd be interested to see an article like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay me, I did a smart! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yay! Someone figured it out (or maybe you're just the first to post it, but still). I'll probably put this up on The Leak, but I kinda wanted to not say it myself before someone else.

This was not my initial intention with the video. At first I was trying to be more emotional, but then during later takes, I aimed for more hopelessness, and realized that it looked a lot like this in the last few iterations of the video. I mostly checked with people to see if I looked defeated, because I felt that evoking that emotion was important, but overall that was sort of the progression I wanted.

It also doesn't help that communicating without communicating is really hard, so I was unsure if people would pick up on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been thinking about this recently, specifically that last video log. I was amazed it existed when I first saw it, so I didn't think about it too much, but on reflection, one has to wonder: what is he doing? He's just kind of mutely staring into the camera. It just sort of reinforces "language is gone now", but it's not terribly interesting all by itself.

Or is it?

He's got enough wherewithal at this point in time to turn a camera on. He knows he wants to do this. What does he does after the camera is on? He stares at it. He can't talk. He can't gesture. Body language is dead. He can't even make facial expressions that might communicate something. This video is an amazing piece of visual storytelling that shows us just how pervasive and destructive this anomaly is, it just takes some thinking. Damn, well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But have they tried communication via scratch and sniff card?!

But seriously, this was exceptional and the audio logs and video gave me chills. Very well done.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fourth and fifth iterations left me speechless. Should I be concerned? O_o

item.title: Re: Have I missed something?
item.content:encoded:
This is a pretty reasonable question - mostly because I don't address it directly. I didn't want to layout the timeline too thoroughly because that kinda hurt the script for that audio file.

The implication is that Gerdinel was just trying to reply to the letters at the time. He wasn't thinking through the whole "I can't believe this is the last thing I'll ever say to them" scenario until the tumult really kicked in. And at that point, cell lines had gone down as part of the whole societal collapse. So at the end of the day, he had the opportunity, just never thought to take it. He had a lot of other things on his mind, and ended up answering the letters because it was part of his routine.

Hopefully that clears that up!

item.title: Re: Have I missed something?
item.content:encoded: Only thing I can think of is that it doesn't effect everyone all at once. So he's still safe until it affects him midway through. I can't review the audio files right now, so I'm basing that on memory.

item.title: Re: Have I missed something?
item.content:encoded: hmmm… That would make sense, except that the speech clearly deteriorates into gibberish at the end and then the next file is him without any form of communication, suggesting that we're reading the SCP in universe and that people don't still have speech and just not understand it.

item.title: Re: Have I missed something?
item.content:encoded: I think we are supposed to be reading the SCP out of universe. Also, speech and writting do still exist, but as a means of communication they don't. Like how he mentions people can gesture but don't understand what it means.

item.title: Re: Have I missed something?
item.content:encoded: But he’s telling us about it in an audiofile and he’s using speech

item.title: Re: Have I missed something?
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure spoken speech was already gone by that point, after using words to write.

item.title: Have I missed something?
item.content:encoded: Why didn't the guy just phone his girlfriend and his family instead of using pictograms?

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: I mean, depending on how Project Foundation goes, there might be no more content on this site. Hopefully I'm not being too prophetic though :P

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
If what you said in your author commentary comes true...
– hide block

Then this wiki should stop producing content in 2026:

"Last update made on 03/14/2026."

I'm going to set a reminder for that day to check.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
The audio is mixed to be really quiet.

edit: oh god the second audio is so much louder

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: CSS is a pain and a half. Nice work o7

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A very good skip. The idea isn't the newest but the execution is fantastic; the audiovisual elements are done perfectly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now this is a K-Class scenario I can get behind. Absolutely loved the ending. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. This is really, really dark, both the article and the idea behind it. +1, definitely.

On a completely unrelated note, I didn't even realize Keter shouldn't be pronounced as Kehter, since it's spelled like Kehter in Russian.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! Actually, stormbreath gave me the idea to make the collapsible a real "gotcha" moment. Originally I had it placed beneath the revision date, but it was their idea to move it and format it like the other links.

Also, related note - it is nontrivial to change the font on collapsible text. Had to learn some CSS to get that looking just right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God, I actually got choked up at parts of this. I saw "it's going to take all communication" coming fairly early but it was executed super well.

This site has a bunch of articles that are structured like "Bad thing, thing gets worse, thing gets worse, thing gets worse, here's how we beat it", so when the last link was just a dropdown and the story ended I felt my heart drop. That's a really good subversion of that trope.

Excellent work overall, +1 easily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if Kate actually followed the instructions of not scripting anything :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved it! I enjoyed the multimedia aspects of it! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perfection. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! I realized that this was going to be incredibly ambitious from the moment I put it up on an ideas and brainstorming thread. Happy that you thought it paid off!

Also, now having done a video myself, I have newfound appreciation for that KateMcTiriss article, because getting one continuous cut like that took me weeks. And if I remember correctly that talking heads portion of the video is a longer clip than mine, so I can only imagine how many trials it took to get that right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ambitious as hell, with a solid payoff. I think recorded video is a new one. (KateMcTiriss has used it, but not quite in the same way.) You should feel proud for pulling this off! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quite possibly a new Series IV favourite. The audio and video are very fitting with the overall narrative. Easy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely a good read for Friday the 13th. One of the more sad SCPs. I love the inclusion of how researchers felt with the audio files.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this. I definitely wasn't expecting the effects of the anomaly to keep building on themselves like that. Instead of just going to "end-of-the-world" you progressed a simple anomaly until you got there, and it was very interesting along the way. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I, the voice of Duboy, pronounced it Keeter at first. Kirby schooled me. I still say Keter like Keeter and not Kehter in general. I don't like Kehter. But technically that is how you say it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow this is amazing, the writing is some of tHe. Best I've ever seen, i love meTA SCPs, great job man. Defiant +1. I haev one question, can you communicate through Morse code?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's finally up! Hooray! Easy plus one, very good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, so "keter" is actually a hebrew word, so this is the correct pronunciation. That doesn't stop me from me reading it to rhyme with "peter" instead of "letter", but the Foundation would almost certainly use the other way.

This was the reaction I got from like half of my beta readers too :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have I been pronouncing Keter wrong the while time? This is the first time I've heard it spoken out-loud.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ooh, the anticipation……

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Language (noun) |langwij|

1. The method of human socialization and correspondence, either spoken or written, consisting of the use of words structured in a conventional way.

2. A barrier to communication.

All spelling, grammar, and other syntactical errors are purposeful. Please do not try to make adjustments to these. If you believe that there is an actual error in the article, send me a PM and I will fix it myself if it is indeed not intentional.

If you require a transcript of the audio and video logs, they can be found here. However, if you are able, I highly recommend reading the article with the multimedia elements, as that is the intended experience.

After almost four months, its finally finished. And there are so many people I need to thank for helping me out with this. There's actually so many that I need to put it in a collapsible to keep this post from getting too long.

Credits
Thank you

First my thanks goes out to DarkStuff, and taylor_itkin for voicing the Duboy and Teller audio files respectively. Not only did they do a fantastic job, but also put up with me bothering them for months about it.

Also thank you to all of the others who tried out for these roles, your enthusiasm and willingness to help was incredibly motivating.

Now I gotta give a shout out to Shaggydredlocks who helped me throughout almost every stage of the process, from the structuring of the article to final touch-ups on the video.

Thirdly, there's just all of the other people who I've bothered for criticism and feedback on this thing:

  • Modern_Erasmus
  • PeppersGhost
  • jabyrwock
  • TheBillith
  • RecursiveRecursion
  • stormbreath
  • DrChandra
  • ratsy
  • Galaxy-Class
  • Varaxous
  • Weryllium
  • Croquembouche
  • LordStonefish
  • Zyn

If I forgot to list you here please PM me and I will add you.

I hope you enjoyed this article. It's been a wild ride making it.

If you want to read more of my work, you can find it here.

And now for some author commentary
AKA why my roommates think I'm insane

This entire article was inspired by the idea of "what would happen if people stopped writing for the wiki (from an in-universe perspective)?" A lot of articles could be considered possible answers to this question. The Foundation could be destroyed, or anomalies could just disappear, or something like that. This article's answer is none of those really. I think that if people stopped writing for the wiki, people would just stop being able to write in-universe. And then I let the idea grow from there.

Now some fun statistics:

  • Number of attempts to record third audio log - 43
  • Number of attempts to record final video log - 94
  • Number of times I had to borrow my roommate's stuff to look like a scientist - 17
  • Number of times I considered cutting the video - 6 (at least)

Other titles I considered for this piece:

  • End-of-Story Scenario
  • Language is a Barrier to Communication
  • A Quiet End
